facts.text: The first regularly scheduled TV broadcasts in the United States began in 1928 by WGY in Schenectady, New York.
The average American watches over four hours of television per day, adding up to about two months of nonstop TV watching per year.
Netflix started as a DVD-by-mail rental service in 1997 before pivoting to streaming in 2007.
The laugh track was invented by sound engineer Charles Douglass in 1953, who built a device called the "laff box" to insert pre-recorded laughter into shows.
The first TV remote control, called "Lazy Bones," was introduced by Zenith in 1950 and was connected to the TV by a wire.
"I Love Lucy" was the first scripted TV show to be filmed in front of a live studio audience using a three-camera setup, a technique still used in sitcoms today.
The Super Bowl is consistently the most-watched television broadcast in the United States, regularly drawing over 100 million viewers.
HBO originally stood for Home Box Office and launched in 1972 as a cable channel showing movies and boxing matches.
The first color TV broadcast in the United States occurred in 1951, but color TVs didn't outsell black-and-white sets until 1972.
"MAS*H" finale in 1983 drew 105.97 million viewers, making it the most-watched single episode of a TV series in U.S. history for decades.
The term "binge-watching" was added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2014, reflecting the cultural shift brought on by streaming platforms.
Disney+ gained over 10 million subscribers within its first 24 hours of launching in November 2019.
The longest-running primetime scripted TV show in the U.S. is "The Simpsons," which premiered in 1989 and has aired over 750 episodes.
The first television advertisement aired in the United States in 1941, a 10-second spot for Bulova watches that cost just $9.
Netflix's recommendation algorithm is estimated to save the company $1 billion per year by reducing subscriber churn.
The Emmy Award statuette depicts a winged woman holding an atom, symbolizing the combination of art and science in television.
"Squid Game" became Netflix's most-watched series ever, reaching 1.65 billion viewing hours in its first 28 days.
The concept of the TV "pilot" comes from the aviation term, as network executives use it to "test fly" a show before committing to a full season.
Hulu was one of the first major streaming services to offer a lower-priced ad-supported tier alongside an ad-free option.
The Nielsen ratings system, which measures TV viewership, was created by Arthur C. Nielsen Sr. in 1950.
"Twin Peaks" is often credited with starting the modern era of prestige television when it premiered on ABC in 1990.
The average cost to produce a single episode of a major streaming original series can exceed $15 million.
Television screens have evolved from cathode ray tubes (CRTs) to plasma, LCD, LED, OLED, and now micro-LED technology.
The first wireless TV remote, the "Flashmatic," used a directional beam of light aimed at photocells on the TV set.
"Game of Thrones" holds the record for the most Emmy Awards won by a drama series, with 59 wins total.
Streaming services collectively spent over $50 billion on content production in 2022 as the streaming wars intensified.
The term "cord-cutting" refers to canceling traditional cable or satellite TV subscriptions in favor of streaming services.
Sony's Trinitron, introduced in 1968, was considered one of the greatest CRT television designs and remained popular for over 30 years.
"Saturday Night Live" has been on the air since 1975, making it one of the longest-running entertainment shows in American TV history.
The first season of "Stranger Things" was rejected by 15 to 20 networks before Netflix picked it up.
4K Ultra HD resolution contains about 8.3 million pixels, four times the resolution of standard 1080p HD.
The iconic "TV dinner" was introduced by Swanson in 1953, designed to be eaten while watching television.
Amazon Prime Video launched in 2006 as Amazon Unbox before being rebranded multiple times.
The first television sets sold in the 1920s and 1930s had tiny screens, often just 5 to 12 inches across.
"Breaking Bad" is often cited by critics as one of the greatest TV dramas ever made, and its popularity surged thanks to Netflix streaming between seasons.
The concept of "appointment television" — watching shows at their scheduled broadcast time — has largely been replaced by on-demand viewing.
South Korea's streaming platform Wavve and Japan's TVer show that streaming wars are a global phenomenon, not just a U.S. trend.
The first live coast-to-coast TV broadcast in the United States occurred in 1951, when President Truman's speech was transmitted via microwave relay.
Smart TVs now account for the majority of television sets sold worldwide, with built-in apps for streaming services.
"The Sopranos" is widely considered the show that launched the golden age of television when it debuted on HBO in 1999.
TV theme songs used to be elaborate compositions lasting a minute or more, but modern shows have shortened or eliminated them to maximize content time.
The average Netflix subscriber watches about 1.5 hours of content per day.
"Seinfeld" was famously described as "a show about nothing," yet it became one of the most profitable sitcoms in TV history.
The first flat-screen plasma TV for consumers was the Fujitsu Plasmavision, released in 1997 at a price of over $14,000.
Reality TV as a genre exploded in the early 2000s with shows like "Survivor" and "American Idol" dominating ratings.
The "golden age of television" is a term that has been used for multiple eras, from the 1950s live dramas to the modern prestige TV boom.
Streaming services use sophisticated compression algorithms like H.265/HEVC to deliver high-quality video while minimizing bandwidth use.
"The Wire" was not a ratings hit during its original HBO run but gained a massive following through DVD and streaming after its conclusion.
TV antennas, also called rabbit ears, work by receiving over-the-air broadcast signals, a technology that still provides free local channels today.
Peacock, NBCUniversal's streaming service, launched in 2020 and is named after NBC's iconic peacock logo.
The blooper reel, a staple of TV comedies, originated in the film industry and was adapted for television in the 1950s.
"Friends" earned its six main cast members $1 million per episode each during the final two seasons, a record at the time.
The DVR (Digital Video Recorder) revolutionized TV viewing when TiVo launched in 1999, letting viewers pause and rewind live TV.
"The Twilight Zone" created by Rod Serling is considered one of the most influential TV series ever, inspiring countless shows and movies.
Streaming resolution depends on internet speed: Netflix recommends at least 5 Mbps for HD and 15 Mbps for 4K Ultra HD.
Cable television was originally developed in the 1940s to bring broadcast signals to rural areas with poor over-the-air reception.
"Jeopardy!" has been on the air since 1964 in various formats and is the longest-running game show in American television.
The concept of "peak TV" was coined by FX CEO John Landgraf in 2015 to describe the overwhelming number of scripted shows being produced.
OLED TV technology allows each pixel to produce its own light, resulting in perfect blacks and infinite contrast ratios.
"The Office" (U.S.) became the most-streamed show on Netflix before moving exclusively to Peacock in 2021.
The first television station to broadcast daily was W2XBS (later WNBC) in New York, which began regular service in 1939.
Apple TV+ launched in 2019 with a focus on original content and a relatively small but critically acclaimed library.
The Nielsen "sweeps" periods in February, May, July, and November were historically when local stations set their advertising rates based on viewership.
"Star Trek" was canceled after just three seasons in 1969 but became a cultural phenomenon through syndicated reruns.
The average TV screen size has grown from about 22 inches in the 1990s to over 55 inches today.
Roku started as a Netflix project to build a streaming hardware device before spinning off as an independent company in 2008.
"The Walking Dead" premiere in 2010 became the most-watched series premiere in cable TV history at the time.
The concept of a "showrunner" — the person who serves as both head writer and executive producer — is unique to television.
Dolby Atmos, used in many streaming originals, creates immersive audio by placing sounds in a three-dimensional space around the viewer.
"Cheers" ran for 11 seasons on NBC from 1982 to 1993 and its finale was watched by over 80 million viewers.
The cable box descrambler was essential in early cable TV, as premium channels like HBO were scrambled to prevent unauthorized viewing.
Paramount+ combined the libraries of CBS All Access and Paramount's film catalog when it launched in 2021.
"The Tonight Show" has been a late-night TV institution since 1954, hosted by Steve Allen, Jack Paar, Johnny Carson, Jay Leno, Conan O'Brien, and Jimmy Fallon.
HDR (High Dynamic Range) technology in modern TVs expands the range of colors and brightness levels displayed on screen.
"Lost" was one of the most expensive TV pilots ever produced, costing an estimated $10 to $14 million in 2004.
The original Universal remote control was invented in the 1980s to reduce the clutter of having separate remotes for each device.
"The Mandalorian" on Disney+ popularized the concept of releasing episodes weekly on a streaming platform rather than all at once.
The term "soap opera" originated because early daytime dramas in the 1930s were sponsored by soap manufacturers like Procter & Gamble.
YouTube TV, Hulu + Live TV, and Sling TV are "virtual MVPDs" — streaming-based alternatives to traditional cable packages.
The closed captioning system for TV was first demonstrated in 1971 and became mandatory on most programming in the 1990s.
"The Oprah Winfrey Show" ran for 25 seasons and is the highest-rated daytime talk show in American TV history.
The V-chip, mandated in all TVs sold in the U.S. since 2000, allows parents to block programming based on content ratings.
"Fleabag" creator and star Phoebe Waller-Bridge adapted the show from a one-woman stage play she performed at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival.
Streaming services track viewer behavior in extraordinary detail, including when you pause, rewind, or abandon a show.
"All in the Family" was a groundbreaking sitcom that tackled controversial social issues like racism, sexism, and homophobia in the 1970s.
The aspect ratio of TV screens shifted from the traditional 4:3 format to widescreen 16:9 to better match the cinematic experience.
Crunchyroll became the dominant streaming platform for anime, amassing over 10 million paid subscribers worldwide.
The concept of the "water cooler show" — a program so popular that coworkers discuss it the next day — has evolved with social media buzz replacing office chatter.
"Mad Men" premiered on AMC in 2007 and helped transform the cable network from a classic movie channel to a prestige TV destination.
The very first TV screens used mechanical spinning disks with holes rather than electronic picture tubes.
Netflix's first original series, "House of Cards," premiered in 2013 and demonstrated that streaming platforms could produce prestige television.
"Gunsmoke" ran for 20 seasons on CBS from 1955 to 1975, making it one of the longest-running primetime live-action series in U.S. history.
Smart TV operating systems like Roku OS, webOS, Tizen, Google TV, and Fire OS now compete as platforms just like mobile phone OS ecosystems.
"The Crown" on Netflix is one of the most expensive TV productions ever, with rumored budgets exceeding $13 million per episode.
The Emergency Broadcast System, later replaced by the Emergency Alert System, has been used on TV since the 1960s to alert viewers of emergencies.
"Frasier" holds the record for the most consecutive Emmy wins for Outstanding Comedy Series, winning five years in a row from 1994 to 1998.
Streaming services have made international content more accessible, with shows like "Dark" (Germany), "Money Heist" (Spain), and "Sacred Games" (India) finding global audiences.
The first televised presidential debate between John F. Kennedy and Richard Nixon in 1960 demonstrated how powerful TV could be in shaping public opinion.
"Arrested Development" was one of the first major TV shows to be revived by a streaming service when Netflix produced its fourth season in 2013.
LED backlighting in LCD TVs replaced older CCFL (Cold Cathode Fluorescent Lamp) technology, making sets thinner and more energy-efficient.
"Downton Abbey" became a global phenomenon for PBS and ITV, proving that period dramas could still captivate massive modern audiences.
The FCC regulates broadcast TV content in the United States but has limited jurisdiction over cable and streaming content.
"The Bachelor" franchise has been on the air since 2002 and has spawned numerous international versions and spinoffs.
Dolby Vision is a proprietary HDR format used by many streaming services to deliver enhanced picture quality on compatible TVs.
"Curb Your Enthusiasm" used a largely improvised format, with creator Larry David providing scene outlines rather than full scripts.
The first handheld TV remote using ultrasonic signals was Zenith's "Space Command" in 1956, which clicked metal bars to change channels.
HBO Max launched in 2020 combining HBO's premium content with WarnerMedia's vast library before rebranding to simply "Max" in 2023.
"The X-Files" popularized serialized storytelling on network TV and created a template for shows that mix episodic and overarching plots.
TV product placement has become a major revenue source, with brands paying shows to feature their products organically in scenes.
"Succession" on HBO won the Emmy for Outstanding Drama Series three times during its four-season run.
The first TV sets with built-in internet connectivity appeared around 2007, laying the groundwork for today's smart TV ecosystem.
"Sesame Street" has been educating children through television since 1969 and has won more Emmy Awards than any other show in history.
Streaming data consumption now accounts for a significant portion of global internet traffic, with Netflix alone using about 15% of all downstream bandwidth.
"The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air" launched Will Smith's acting career and ran for six seasons on NBC from 1990 to 1996.
QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation) tuners in TVs allow reception of unencrypted digital cable channels without a cable box.
"Bridgerton" on Netflix became the platform's most-watched series at the time of its release, blending period drama with modern sensibilities.
The concept of "channel surfing" became popular with the widespread adoption of remote controls in the 1980s.
"Buffy the Vampire Slayer" is credited with pioneering long-form serialized storytelling in genre television during the late 1990s.
MicroLED technology promises to combine the best qualities of OLED and LCD, offering self-emissive pixels without the risk of burn-in.
"The Daily Show" transformed from a comedy program into one of the most trusted news sources for younger viewers under Jon Stewart's tenure.
The SAP (Secondary Audio Program) button on older TV remotes allowed access to alternate language audio tracks on broadcast television.
"Yellowstone" became the most-watched show on cable television in over a decade, drawing over 12 million viewers per episode at its peak.
Streaming companies use A/B testing to optimize everything from thumbnail images to episode descriptions to maximize viewer engagement.
"I Love Lucy" star Lucille Ball became the first woman to run a major television studio, Desilu Productions, in 1962.
HDMI (High-Definition Multimedia Interface) replaced older connections like composite, S-Video, and component cables for connecting devices to TVs.
"Schitt's Creek" swept all seven major comedy categories at the 2020 Emmy Awards, a feat never accomplished before.
The global streaming market is projected to exceed $200 billion in annual revenue, driven by subscription and advertising models.
"Roots" miniseries in 1977 drew an estimated 130 million viewers for its finale, making it one of the most-watched TV events ever.
Quantum dot technology in Samsung's QLED TVs uses nanocrystals to produce more vibrant and accurate colors than standard LED displays.
"Black Mirror" started as a British Channel 4 series before being acquired by Netflix, where it became a global phenomenon exploring technology's dark side.
The three major broadcast networks — ABC, CBS, and NBC — once commanded over 90% of the TV audience but now share the market with hundreds of channels and platforms.
"Ted Lasso" on Apple TV+ won the Emmy for Outstanding Comedy Series in its first two seasons and helped legitimize Apple's streaming service.
TV signal transmission shifted from analog to digital in the United States in 2009, improving picture and sound quality while freeing up radio spectrum.
"Survivor" has been on the air since 2000 and is considered one of the shows that launched the reality TV genre in America.
Refresh rate, measured in hertz (Hz), determines how smoothly motion appears on a TV — most modern sets offer 60Hz or 120Hz native refresh rates.
"The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel" on Amazon Prime Video won eight Emmy Awards in its first season, the most ever for a comedy season debut.
The term "rabbit ears" for TV antennas comes from the V-shaped dipole antennas that sit on top of TV sets and resemble a rabbit's ears.
"Stranger Things" Season 4 broke Netflix records by accumulating over 1.15 billion hours of viewing time in its first 28 days.
Picture-in-picture (PiP) technology, which lets you watch two channels simultaneously, was first introduced on TVs in the mid-1970s.
"Rick and Morty" became one of the highest-rated animated series among adults, rivaling long-running shows like "The Simpsons" and "South Park."
Streaming services have adopted variable bitrate encoding, which adjusts video quality dynamically based on the complexity of each scene.
"WKRP in Cincinnati" featured one of TV's most iconic Thanksgiving episodes involving a live turkey drop gone wrong.
The coaxial cable used for cable TV was originally developed for telephone and telegraph communications in the early 20th century.
"Cobra Kai" started as a YouTube Premium original before moving to Netflix, where it became one of the platform's most popular series.
The average household subscribes to four or more streaming services, spending roughly $50 to $100 per month on streaming alone.
"The West Wing" popularized the "walk and talk" filming technique, where characters deliver dialogue while moving through hallways.
ATSC 3.0, also known as NextGen TV, is the newest broadcast standard in the U.S. and supports 4K, HDR, and interactive features over the air.
"Fleabag" broke the fourth wall as a storytelling device, with the main character frequently looking directly at the camera to address the audience.
Chromecast, Apple TV, Amazon Fire Stick, and Roku devices are known as streaming media players or "dongles" that plug into a TV's HDMI port.
"ER" aired for 15 seasons on NBC and launched the career of George Clooney, who starred in the show's early years.
The phrase "stay tuned" originated in early radio and television when viewers had to physically tune their sets to the correct frequency.
"Severance" on Apple TV+ received widespread critical acclaim and became a cultural talking point for its exploration of work-life balance themes.
TV screen burn-in occurs when a static image is displayed for too long, leaving a permanent ghost image, particularly on plasma and OLED screens.
"Law & Order: SVU" is the longest-running live-action primetime TV series in U.S. history, surpassing the original "Law & Order" and "Gunsmoke."
Ad-supported streaming tiers have become increasingly common as services like Netflix, Disney+, and HBO Max seek additional revenue.
"The Honeymooners" starring Jackie Gleason is considered one of the greatest TV comedies ever despite lasting only one season in its classic form.
CRT TVs could weigh over 200 pounds for larger models, while modern flat-panel TVs of the same screen size weigh a fraction of that.
"Arcane" on Netflix, based on the video game "League of Legends," demonstrated that animated adaptations of games could achieve critical success.
The "skip intro" button, popularized by Netflix, was developed after data showed most viewers skipped show openings when possible.
"Columbo" was unique for a detective show because the audience always knew who the killer was from the start of each episode.
Frame interpolation, or motion smoothing, on modern TVs adds artificially generated frames to make content look smoother, but many filmmakers dislike the effect.
"The Bear" on Hulu received universal critical acclaim and won multiple Emmy Awards for its intense portrayal of life in a restaurant kitchen.
Nielsen expanded its measurements to include streaming viewership data starting in 2017 to keep up with changing media consumption habits.
"Married... with Children" was the first primetime series to air on the then-new Fox network in 1987.
Local dimming technology in LED TVs selectively dims different zones of the backlight to improve contrast and reduce light bleeding.
"Wednesday" on Netflix became one of the platform's biggest hits, drawing over 1 billion viewing hours in its first weeks.
The term "television" combines the Greek word "tele" (far) and the Latin word "visio" (sight), literally meaning "far sight."
"Dexter" was one of Showtime's most successful series and was revived as "Dexter: New Blood" nearly a decade after its controversial finale.
Streaming platforms now release viewership data more openly, with Netflix publishing weekly top-10 lists by hours viewed.
"Happy Days" ran for 11 seasons and spawned multiple spinoffs, including "Laverne & Shirley" and "Mork & Mindy."
The soundbar became popular as flat-panel TVs got thinner, since their slim profiles left little room for quality built-in speakers.
"Peaky Blinders" gained a massive global following after being added to Netflix, despite originally airing on BBC Two.
The binge-release model, where all episodes drop at once, was pioneered by Netflix but has been debated as it can reduce prolonged cultural conversation.
"Bewitched" ran from 1964 to 1972 and controversially replaced its lead actor Darrin, played by Dick York, with Dick Sargent midway through the series.
eARC (Enhanced Audio Return Channel) in modern TVs allows high-quality audio formats like Dolby Atmos to be passed from the TV to a soundbar or receiver via HDMI.
"Only Murders in the Building" on Hulu became one of the most critically acclaimed comedy series in recent years.
Over 80% of U.S. households now subscribe to at least one streaming video service.
"Murphy Brown" sparked a real political controversy when Vice President Dan Quayle criticized the character's decision to be a single mother in 1992.
VHS tapes once allowed viewers to record TV shows for the first time, a concept upheld by the Supreme Court in the 1984 "Betamax case."
"Lupin" became one of the first French-language series to become a global hit on Netflix, reaching tens of millions of viewers worldwide.
The channel-up/channel-down buttons on TV remotes were among the first features of early remote controls.
"Fargo" on FX proved that a TV anthology series could successfully expand on a beloved film while standing on its own.
Streaming services now produce content in dozens of languages, with subtitles and dubbing making shows accessible to global audiences.
"Dragon Ball Z" was one of the anime series that introduced Western audiences to Japanese animation through TV syndication in the 1990s.
The concept of "second screen" refers to viewers using a phone or tablet while watching TV, often to browse related content or social media.
"Mare of Easttown" on HBO demonstrated the continued appeal of limited series, winning Kate Winslet an Emmy for her lead performance.
The FCC's "must-carry" rules require cable systems to carry local broadcast stations, ensuring viewers don't lose access to free channels.
"Euphoria" on HBO became a cultural touchstone for Gen Z viewers and was noted for its innovative cinematography and visual style.
TV sets now regularly come with voice assistants built in, including Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant, and Apple's Siri.
"The Jeffersons" was one of the first TV shows to feature an affluent African American family and ran for 11 seasons.
The FAST (Free Ad-Supported Streaming Television) model has grown rapidly with services like Pluto TV, Tubi, and the Roku Channel.
"Chernobyl" on HBO became the highest-rated series on IMDb shortly after its premiere, earning a 9.4 out of 10 rating.
TV signal boosters and amplifiers help strengthen weak over-the-air signals for viewers who live far from broadcast towers.
"Veep" won Julia Louis-Dreyfus six consecutive Emmy Awards for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Comedy Series, a record for any performer.
The streaming industry has created new metrics like "completion rate" and "hours viewed" to replace traditional TV ratings.
"Roseanne" was groundbreaking for depicting a working-class American family and returned for a revival in 2018 before being canceled.
HDMI 2.1, found in newer TVs, supports features like 4K at 120Hz, variable refresh rate (VRR), and auto low latency mode (ALLM) for gaming.
"The Last of Us" on HBO became one of the most successful video game adaptations in TV history, drawing massive audiences and critical praise.
Bunny ears and rooftop antennas can still receive free over-the-air HDTV broadcasts in digital format from local stations.
"How I Met Your Mother" ran for nine seasons, and its divisive series finale remains one of the most debated endings in sitcom history.
Streaming services own less and less of their content libraries as studios pull back titles for their own platforms.
"Dragnet" in the 1950s was one of the earliest police procedural TV shows and established many conventions of the genre.
Mini-LED backlighting uses thousands of smaller LEDs compared to standard LED, allowing for more precise local dimming zones and better contrast.
"Andor" on Disney+ received critical acclaim as one of the best "Star Wars" TV series, praised for its mature and nuanced storytelling.
The average TV commercial break during primetime is about 14 to 16 minutes per hour of programming on broadcast networks.
"The Cosby Show" was the number one rated show in America for five consecutive seasons in the 1980s.
Content delivery networks (CDNs) are the backbone of streaming, distributing video data across servers worldwide to reduce buffering and latency.
"Narcos" on Netflix helped popularize international true-crime dramas and featured dialogue in both English and Spanish.
The picture tube in old CRT televisions worked by firing electron beams at phosphorescent dots on the screen to create an image.
"It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia" became the longest-running live-action comedy series in American TV history.
Password sharing crackdowns by Netflix and other streamers have been a major industry development, pushing subscribers to pay for additional accounts.
"Bonanza" was the first regularly broadcast TV series shown in color and ran for 14 seasons on NBC.
TV input lag, the delay between pressing a button and seeing its effect on screen, is critical for gamers and typically measures 10 to 30 milliseconds on modern sets.
"The Witcher" on Netflix demonstrated the appetite for fantasy adaptations beyond "Game of Thrones."
Live TV streaming services like YouTube TV and Hulu + Live TV offer cloud DVR storage, letting users record broadcast content to watch later.
"LOST" is credited with helping popularize fan theory culture online, as viewers obsessively analyzed clues and debated plot points between episodes.
The test pattern — a geometric image displayed when no programming was airing — was once a common sight on TV sets, especially late at night.
"Ozark" on Netflix launched Jason Bateman's dramatic career and ran for four critically acclaimed seasons.
Most streaming services now offer offline download capability, allowing subscribers to watch content without an internet connection.
"M*A*S*H" set in the Korean War actually ran longer than the war itself — the show lasted 11 seasons while the war lasted about three years.
The aspect ratio of early TV was inspired by the Academy ratio used in films of the 1930s and 1940s.
"Abbott Elementary" on ABC became a breakout network comedy hit, proving that broadcast TV could still launch culturally relevant new shows.
Adaptive streaming technology automatically adjusts video quality based on the viewer's internet connection speed, preventing buffering.
"Dallas" cliffhanger "Who Shot J.R.?" captivated audiences in 1980 and became one of the most famous moments in TV history.
TV manufacturers now include gaming-specific features like ALLM, VRR, and FreeSync or G-Sync compatibility to attract gamers.
"Watchmen" on HBO was originally envisioned as a limited series and won 11 Emmy Awards including Outstanding Limited Series.
The proliferation of streaming services has led to concerns about "subscription fatigue" among consumers juggling multiple monthly bills.
"Taxi" ran from 1978 to 1983 and won 18 Emmy Awards during its run despite struggling with ratings.
Edge-lit LED TVs position their LEDs along the edges of the panel instead of directly behind it, allowing for thinner designs.
"Loki" on Disney+ became the most-watched premiere on the platform when it debuted and explored the multiverse concept for Marvel.
The term "television" was first used at the 1900 World's Fair in Paris, though practical TV was still decades away.
"Baywatch" was one of the most widely viewed TV series in the world during the 1990s, airing in over 140 countries.
Streaming wars have led to content being removed and relocated between platforms as licensing deals expire and studios launch their own services.
"The Mary Tyler Moore Show" was groundbreaking for featuring a single, career-focused woman as its lead character in the 1970s.
Pixel density, measured in pixels per inch (PPI), determines how sharp an image appears, and is particularly important for viewing distance.
"Reacher" on Amazon Prime Video became one of the platform's most-watched original series globally.
The "broadcast flag" was a proposed system to prevent digital TV recordings from being redistributed, but the FCC's mandate was struck down in court.
"The Handmaid's Tale" on Hulu was the first streaming-exclusive series to win the Emmy for Outstanding Drama Series in 2017.
Plasma TVs, once prized for their deep blacks and wide viewing angles, were discontinued by all major manufacturers by 2014.
"House" ran for eight seasons and was one of the most-watched shows globally during its run, airing in over 60 countries.
IPTV (Internet Protocol Television) delivers TV content through internet protocols rather than traditional broadcast or cable methods.
"Community" coined the phrase "six seasons and a movie," which became a rallying cry for fan campaigns to save beloved shows.
TV watching peaks during the winter months and drops during the summer, a pattern that originally influenced the broadcast season schedule.
"Homeland" was adapted from the Israeli series "Prisoners of War" and won the Emmy for Outstanding Drama Series in its first season.
Motion blur on TVs occurs when fast-moving images appear smeared or fuzzy, and is addressed by higher refresh rates and motion processing.
"Black-ish" became one of ABC's most acclaimed sitcoms for its exploration of race, identity, and family in modern America.
The average streaming subscriber is now more likely to cancel and re-subscribe to services based on specific content releases, a trend called "churn and return."
"The Partridge Family" was one of several TV shows built around a musical act, with star David Cassidy becoming a teen idol.
HEVC (High Efficiency Video Coding) and AV1 are competing video codecs used by streaming services to compress video data more efficiently.
"Succession" on HBO depicted the power struggles within a wealthy media family and was often compared to the real-life Murdoch dynasty.
Set-top boxes from cable and satellite providers are increasingly being replaced by streaming devices and smart TV apps.
"Malcolm in the Middle" pioneered the single-camera comedy format for sitcoms when many peers still used multi-camera setups with laugh tracks.
TV rating systems like TV-MA, TV-14, and TV-PG were established by the TV Parental Guidelines in 1997 to inform viewers about content.
"Outer Banks" on Netflix became a youth-oriented hit and was renewed for multiple seasons based on strong viewership.
The invention of the transistor in 1947 led to the creation of portable, lightweight televisions in the 1960s.
"Gilmore Girls" was revived by Netflix with "A Year in the Life" in 2016, demonstrating the streaming platform's ability to resurrect fan-favorite shows.
AI is increasingly used by streaming platforms to generate personalized thumbnails, predict hit shows, and even assist in content creation.
"Good Times" was the first sitcom to feature a two-parent African American family and dealt with serious issues like poverty and gang violence.
Contrast ratio on a TV measures the difference between the brightest white and darkest black the display can produce simultaneously.
"Stranger Things" on Netflix was inspired by 1980s pop culture, with creators the Duffer Brothers citing Steven Spielberg and Stephen King as major influences.
The shift from weekly episode releases to binge drops has changed how TV shows are written, with greater emphasis on cliffhangers and season-long arcs.
"Hill Street Blues" revolutionized the police drama genre in 1981 with its ensemble cast, serialized storylines, and gritty realism.
Viewing habits data reveals that most binge-watchers finish a series within a week of starting it.
"Northern Exposure" won the Emmy for Outstanding Drama Series in 1992 despite airing on CBS, which was considered an underdog network at the time.
Screen mirroring technologies like AirPlay and Miracast allow viewers to wirelessly send content from their phones or laptops to a TV.
"Invincible" on Amazon Prime Video proved that adult animated superhero shows could compete with live-action adaptations.
TV audience measurement is transitioning from sample-based panels to "big data" approaches that track every individual viewer's habits.
"That '70s Show" ran for eight seasons and is credited with launching the careers of Ashton Kutcher, Mila Kunis, and Topher Grace.
Wide color gamut (WCG) technology in modern TVs can display a broader range of colors than older sets, making images more lifelike.
"Mindhunter" on Netflix, produced by David Fincher, explored the early days of FBI criminal profiling and was praised for its meticulous attention to detail.
The "upfronts" are annual events where broadcast networks present their upcoming fall schedules to advertisers, a tradition dating back to the 1960s.
"The Muppet Show" ran from 1976 to 1981 and was watched by an estimated 235 million viewers worldwide at its peak.
TVs with built-in Chromecast or AirPlay support have reduced the need for separate streaming devices.
"White Lotus" on HBO became a cultural phenomenon and won 10 Emmy Awards across its first two seasons.
The standard broadcast TV channels in the United States range from channel 2 to channel 36 in the VHF and UHF frequency bands.
"Alias" starring Jennifer Garner was one of the first network shows to incorporate complex serialized mythology similar to what streaming shows later popularized.
Cloud gaming services integrated into smart TVs are blurring the line between television and gaming consoles.
"30 Rock" won three consecutive Emmy Awards for Outstanding Comedy Series and is considered one of the sharpest TV satires ever made.
The coax-to-HDMI transition in home entertainment required many viewers to upgrade both their cables and viewing equipment simultaneously.
"Prehistoric Planet" on Apple TV+ showcased how streaming budgets could rival or exceed those of major documentary productions by traditional networks.
The Writers Guild and SAG-AFTRA strikes of 2023 centered heavily on streaming residuals and the use of AI in content creation.
"Sanford and Son" was adapted from the British sitcom "Steptoe and Son" and became one of the highest-rated shows of the 1970s.
AV1 is an open-source, royalty-free video codec developed by the Alliance for Open Media, designed to compete with proprietary formats like HEVC.
"Physical" on Apple TV+ explored the 1980s aerobics craze and was praised for its dark comedic tone.
The proliferation of content across multiple platforms has given rise to aggregator services like JustWatch and Reelgood that help viewers find where to stream shows.
"Designing Women" was known for its sharp, politically charged monologues delivered by Dixie Carter's character, Julia Sugarbaker.
Lag-free wireless HDMI transmitters and receivers have made it easier for TV setups to be cleaner without visible cable runs.
"The Rehearsal" on HBO, created by Nathan Fielder, pushed the boundaries of reality-based television with its unique premise.
The U.S. transition from Standard Definition to High Definition broadcasting required millions of viewers to either upgrade their TVs or purchase converter boxes.
"LA Law" is credited with helping establish the legal drama as a major TV genre and ran for eight seasons on NBC.
Spatial audio on streaming apps creates an immersive listening experience using headphones, especially on services like Apple TV+ and Netflix.
"Severance" was conceived by first-time showrunner Dan Erickson, who developed the script while working as an assistant to TV producer Kari Lizer.
Interactive TV content, like Netflix's "Black Mirror: Bandersnatch," lets viewers make choices that affect the storyline.
"Taxi Driver" composer Bernard Herrmann also scored several classic TV shows, bridging the worlds of film and television music.
TV bezels — the frame around the screen — have shrunk dramatically, with many modern sets featuring nearly bezel-free designs.
"Penny Dreadful" on Showtime blended classic horror literature characters into a single narrative universe before the concept became a streaming trend.
The average American home now has more streaming subscriptions than people living in it.
"Reno 911!" was revived on the short-lived streaming service Quibi before moving to Roku, showcasing how shows can migrate between platforms.
TV resolution has evolved from 480i (standard definition) through 720p, 1080i, 1080p, 4K, and now 8K.
"The Americans" on FX was praised as one of the best spy dramas ever made but never achieved high ratings, becoming a classic example of a critically loved but underwatched series.
Voice search on smart TVs and streaming devices has largely replaced the tedious process of typing with an on-screen keyboard via remote.
"Bluey" on Disney+ became one of the most popular children's shows in the world and is praised for its appeal to both kids and adults.
Cable networks originally didn't have commercials and were subscription-only, but most eventually adopted advertising to increase revenue.
"Six Feet Under" on HBO was lauded for its series finale, which many consider one of the greatest endings in TV history.
Backlight bleed, where light leaks around the edges of an LCD TV screen, is a common issue with edge-lit LED panels.
"Killing Eve" on BBC America revitalized the spy thriller genre on TV with its cat-and-mouse dynamic between Sandra Oh and Jodie Comer.
Multiple streaming services now offer live sporting events, with Amazon Prime Video, Apple TV+, and Peacock all securing major sports rights.
"Beavis and Butt-Head" was a controversial animated hit on MTV in the 1990s, known for its crude humor and social commentary.
TV manufacturers have introduced features like ambient mode, where the TV displays artwork or blends with the wall when not in use.
"Beef" on Netflix became an unexpected hit and won multiple Emmy Awards, showcasing the potential of limited series on streaming.
The standard frame rate for U.S. television has been 29.97 frames per second (NTSC) since the transition to color TV in the 1950s.
"The Good Place" used philosophy and ethics as the foundation of its comedy, bringing academic concepts to a mainstream audience.
Streaming services have given new life to catalog content, with classic shows like "The Golden Girls" and "Seinfeld" finding new audiences decades later.
"Gilligan's Island" ran for only three seasons but became one of the most enduring shows through decades of syndicated reruns.
OLED TVs can be made paper-thin because they don't require a backlight — each pixel generates its own light.
"Slow Horses" on Apple TV+ proved that espionage thrillers remain a viable genre for streaming platforms.
The introduction of picture-quality settings like "Filmmaker Mode" preserves the director's intended look by disabling motion smoothing and adjustments.
"Dynasty" and "Dallas" were premier examples of 1980s primetime soap operas, filled with glamorous settings and dramatic plot twists.
Streaming services have reshaped the movie industry, with many theatrical releases debuting simultaneously or exclusively on streaming platforms.
"Battlestar Galactica" (2004 reimagining) is frequently cited as one of the best science fiction TV series ever produced.
Energy Star certification for TVs means the set meets EPA standards for energy efficiency, consuming less power during operation and standby.
"Pachinko" on Apple TV+ was praised for its multilingual storytelling, seamlessly shifting between Korean, Japanese, and English.
The practice of releasing TV show trailers during major events like the Super Bowl has become a key marketing strategy.
"Wonder Years" was notable for using an adult narrator reflecting on childhood, a format that influenced many subsequent coming-of-age shows.
TV manufacturers now offer lifestyle TVs that double as decorative elements, with Samsung's The Frame and LG's Gallery series leading the trend.
"Dopesick" on Hulu dramatized the opioid crisis and was notable for blending true events with dramatized storytelling.
The launch of each new streaming service has been accompanied by exclusive content deals that pull shows off rival platforms.
"Night Court" was a popular NBC sitcom in the 1980s that was revived for a new generation in 2023.
Burn-in protection features like pixel shifting and screen savers are built into OLED TVs to mitigate the risk of permanent image retention.
"Shogun" on FX/Hulu became a massive critical and commercial hit, winning a record-breaking 18 Emmy Awards for its first season.
The introduction of HDTV broadcasting in the late 1990s was one of the most significant technological shifts in television history.
"Full House" ran from 1987 to 1995 and was brought back as "Fuller House" on Netflix for five additional seasons.
Streaming quality can vary significantly between devices — a budget smart TV may not support Dolby Vision or Atmos even when the service offers them.
"True Detective" Season 1 is considered one of the finest seasons of television ever produced, starring Matthew McConaughey and Woody Harrelson.
TV advertising revenue in the U.S. totals over $60 billion annually, though an increasing share is shifting to connected TV and streaming ads.
"The Addams Family" TV show ran for only two seasons in the 1960s but became a pop culture institution that spawned films and a Netflix series.
Progressive scan (1080p) displays the entire frame at once, while interlaced (1080i) alternates between odd and even lines, giving progressive a smoother look.
"House of the Dragon" on HBO became the most-watched premiere in the network's history and helped fill the void left by "Game of Thrones."
Fiber optic internet has made 4K and even 8K streaming practical for many households that previously struggled with buffering.
"Three's Company" was based on the British sitcom "Man About the House" and became one of the most popular shows of the late 1970s.
The number of scripted original series produced annually in the U.S. peaked at 599 in 2022 before declining as studios cut costs.
"Better Call Saul" is considered one of the rare TV spinoffs that equals or surpasses the quality of its parent series, "Breaking Bad."
Viewing angle performance varies by TV technology — OLED and IPS LCD panels maintain color accuracy at wider angles than VA panels.
"Boardwalk Empire" on HBO brought Prohibition-era Atlantic City to life with lavish production values and a cast led by Steve Buscemi.
Parental controls on streaming services allow parents to set content restrictions, create kid profiles, and limit screen time.
"The Brady Bunch" has been continuously in syndication since 1975, making it one of the most rerun shows in TV history.
8K resolution (7680 x 4320 pixels) offers four times the detail of 4K, but there is currently very little native 8K content available.
"Beef" on Netflix demonstrated the potential for Asian American storytelling in mainstream television.
TV measurement company Comscore competes with Nielsen by using set-top box data from cable and satellite providers to track viewership.
"King of the Hill" produced by Mike Judge ran for 13 seasons and was known for its subtle, observational humor.
Smart TV platforms now serve as content aggregation hubs, recommending shows across multiple streaming services from a single interface.
"Pose" on FX was groundbreaking for featuring the largest cast of transgender actors in regular roles in TV history.
The cathode ray tube was invented by Karl Ferdinand Braun in 1897, but it took decades before CRT technology was used in television sets.
"Reservation Dogs" on FX was the first TV series to feature an entirely Indigenous North American writing team and production staff.
TV-connected devices like gaming consoles (PlayStation, Xbox) serve as de facto streaming boxes for many households.
"The Bob Newhart Show" is famous for its series finale, in which the entire show was revealed to be a dream from Newhart's earlier series.
Ambient light sensors in modern TVs automatically adjust screen brightness based on the room's lighting conditions for optimal viewing.
"Lessons in Chemistry" on Apple TV+ was adapted from the bestselling novel and starred Brie Larson.
The concept of "appointment viewing" has returned somewhat with weekly release shows generating social media buzz each episode.
"Knight Rider" starring David Hasselhoff and featuring a talking car named KITT became an iconic 1980s TV show.
Wi-Fi 6 and Wi-Fi 6E connectivity in smart TVs allows for faster, more reliable streaming with less buffering in congested home networks.
"Fallout" on Amazon Prime Video became one of the most-watched series on the platform, proving video game adaptations could work.
The SAG Award for Outstanding Ensemble in a Comedy/Drama Series has become a reliable predictor of Emmy and Oscar success.
"The Nanny" starring Fran Drescher ran for six seasons and remains beloved for its fashion, humor, and Drescher's distinctive voice.
Dual-cell LCD technology uses two LCD layers to dramatically improve contrast ratio performance.
"Mr. Robot" on USA Network was praised for its realistic portrayal of hacking and cybersecurity, unusual for a mainstream TV show.
International co-productions between streaming services and local broadcasters have become a major model for global content creation.
"Dawson's Creek" was one of the first teen dramas to build a passionate online fanbase, anticipating the role of internet fandom in TV culture.
TV resolution is rapidly approaching the limits of what the human eye can distinguish at normal viewing distances.
"Catastrophe" on Amazon Prime Video was one of the first critically acclaimed original series for the platform.
Cable TV subscriber numbers have declined steadily since their peak of about 100 million U.S. households in 2012.
"Diff'rent Strokes" ran from 1978 to 1986 and spawned the catchphrase "Whatchu talkin' 'bout, Willis?"
Transparent OLED and rollable TV screens represent the cutting edge of display technology.
"For All Mankind" on Apple TV+ reimagines history through an alternate timeline where the space race never ended.
The average length of a TV drama episode has increased from the standard 42-44 minutes on broadcast to 50-70 minutes on streaming platforms.
"Sanford and Son" star Redd Foxx's signature fake heart attacks on the show became one of TV's most memorable running gags.
Streaming services routinely cancel shows after one or two seasons if viewership doesn't meet targets, frustrating fans invested in ongoing storylines.
